When a Fiori application reads a CDS view backed by such an association, it automatically appends a WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) clause to the generated SQL query, passing the current user's session locale as the parameter value.

SELECT *
FROM MY_CDS_VIEW
WITH PARAMETERS ('LOCALE' = 'he')

This instructs HANA to join the text table and return only the rows matching the Hebrew language key.
Without this parameter, the CDS view would either return texts for all languages or fall back to a system default, neither of which is appropriate for a user-facing Fiori application.

Example — Hebrew locale in action

A product or entity search CDS view with LOCALE = 'he' returns Hebrew descriptions:

KeyDescription
ITEM-001צינור פלדה (Steel Pipe)
ITEM-002ספק ראשי בע"מ (Main Supplier Ltd.)
ITEM-003חברת דוגמה בע"מ (Example Company Ltd.)

The Problem: HEX Engine Limitation

SAP HANA's HEX (High-Performance Analytical Execution) Engine is the modern columnar execution engine optimized for CDS views accessed from Fiori applications.
It delivers substantially lower CPU consumption compared to the fallback Column Engine, particularly for queries involving joins, projections, and filters typical of CDS-based reads.

However, as documented in SAP Note 3513507, the HEX engine has a known limitation: it cannot process queries containing a WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) clause.
When such a query arrives, HANA silently falls back to the Column Engine.

Why this goes undetected: This fallback is entirely invisible during development and unit testing.
Results are correct - just processed less efficiently.
The impact only becomes apparent in production, where many users simultaneously access the same Fiori apps in a non-English locale.

This is particularly relevant in any deployment where the working language differs from the system installation default - including Hebrew, Arabic, Japanese, Chinese, Thai, and others.

The Solution: Adding the HEX Hint

There are two approaches, and the correct choice depends on whether you own the CDS view or not.
 
Prerequisite — must be applied before either option below
By default, SAP HANA excludes queries containing `WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?)` from HEX engine execution. Setting 'hex_enable_with_parameters_locale = true' in 'indexserver.ini' removes this restriction at the system level - without it, any hint applied at the view or statement level will have no effect on LOCALE queries.

Requires SAP HANA Revision >= 075 (SPS07).
Do not apply on SAP HANA <= 2.00.074 — see SAP Note 3513507.
ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('indexserver.ini', 'SYSTEM')
SET ('sql','hex_enable_with_parameters_locale') = 'true'
WITH RECONFIGURE COMMENT 'SAP Note 3513507';

ALTER SYSTEM CLEAR SQL PLAN CACHE;

Option 1 — Preferred: Add the Hint Directly in the CDS View

If the CDS view is a custom view that you own and can modify, the preferred approach is to embed the DB hint directly in the CDS view definition using a consumption annotation. This is the clean, transport-safe, source-controlled solution described in SAP Note 2717265.
In the CDS view DDL, add the hint annotation:
@DatabaseHint: [{dbSystem: #HDB, hint: 'USE_HEX_PLAN'}]
define view entity YOUR_CDS_VIEW ...​
Or in the ABAP SELECT consuming the view, use the %_HINTS addition:
SELECT *
  FROM your_cds_view
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
  %_HINTS HDB 'USE_HEX_PLAN'.
This approach keeps the optimization explicit, versioned, and visible in the development layer.

Option 2 — Fallback: ANNOTATION Hint at the HANA Layer

If the CDS view is a standard SAP-delivered view that you cannot modify, or if you need a temporary fix while a proper code change is being prepared, you can apply the hint at the HANA database layer using the ANNOTATION mechanism (SAP Note 3076534).
 
Important: This should not be the first choice. It is a workaround - applied at the database layer outside the ABAP development lifecycle.
Use it only when Option 1 is not applicable.
 
-- Apply the hint
ANNOTATE "YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME" SET 'HINT' = 'USE_HEX_PLAN';

-- Confirm the annotation is registered
SELECT * FROM ANNOTATIONS;

-- Clear the SQL Plan Cache so HANA generates a fresh execution plan
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

To roll back:

ANNOTATE "YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME" UNSET 'HINT';

Regardless of which option is used, verify the result the same way:

SELECT EXECUTION_ENGINE, STATEMENT_HASH
FROM M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE
WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

The EXECUTION_ENGINE column should now show HEX.

Large IN-List Conditions from ABAP Range Tables

SAP Note 2399993 -  SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)

The Problem: How ABAP Generates Thousands of SQL Filter Conditions

A common ABAP pattern for reading data from a CDS view is to build a range table and pass it using the IN operator:

SELECT *
FROM zcds_my_view
INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
WHERE key_field IN @LR_range.

This is clean, readable ABAP - but it has a critical hidden cost at the database layer.
When ABAP's DB interface translates this statement into SQL, it expands the range table into a series of explicit filter conditions:

SELECT * FROM ZCDS_MY_VIEW
WHERE KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE001'
   OR KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE002'
   OR KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE003'
   -- ... potentially thousands more conditions

If lr_range contains hundreds or thousands of entries - entirely realistic for document processing, purchase order workflows, or batch jobs - HANA must parse and compile this statement each time it is executed.
This compilation overhead, not the data retrieval itself, is what drives CPU spikes and degraded response times.

Why this goes undetected: In development and testing, range tables are typically small (a handful of test records).
The problem scales silently with production data volumes, only surfacing once the system is under real load.

This pattern is additionally harmful because:

  • It generates near-identical but technically distinct SQL statements (different values each run), preventing effective plan cache reuse.
  • It worsens over time as data volumes and document counts grow.

The Solution: FOR ALL ENTRIES with HANA FDA Hint

Note: FDA for FOR ALL ENTRIES is enabled by default on ABAP kernel >= 7.45 (dbs/hdb/prefer_join_with_fda = 1).
The hint &prefer_join_with_fda 1& in the examples below is a per-statement control - useful for targeted application or troubleshooting - not a feature activation.
If you are on a modern kernel, the mechanism is already active system-wide.

Replace the IN operator with FOR ALL ENTRIES IN, combined with a HANA Fast Data Access (FDA) hint:

SELECT *
  FROM zcds_my_view
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
  FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @LR_range
  WHERE key_field = @LR_range-low
  %_HINTS HDB '&prefer_join_with_fda 1&'.

Why This Works: SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)

FDA is a SAP HANA optimization specifically designed for FOR ALL ENTRIES queries. Instead of expanding the input list into SQL filter conditions, HANA:

  1. Loads the input list into a HANA-managed temporary table in memory.
  2. Joins the CDS view result against this temporary table.
  3. Lets the HANA query optimizer determine the most efficient join strategy.

The result is a single, stable, reusable SQL execution plan regardless of how many entries are in the input list.
Compilation overhead is eliminated, plan cache reuse is maximized, and CPU consumption is substantially reduced.

Comparison: IN vs FOR ALL ENTRIES + FDA

AspectIN '@LR_range'FOR ALL ENTRIES + FDA
SQL generatedOne condition per range entrySingle join against temp table
Compile timeGrows with range sizeConstant
Plan cache reuseLow (unique SQL per execution)High (stable plan)
CPU impact at scaleHighLow
ABAP complexityMinimalSlightly higher

 

Important considerations for FOR ALL ENTRIES: If the input table is empty, FOR ALL ENTRIES returns no rows, unlike IN which would return all rows in that case.
It also implicitly removes duplicate rows from the result set.
Ensure your application logic accounts for both behaviors before applying this change.
 

General Best Practices

Always Test in a Non-Production System First

Both fixes involve changes at the database or ABAP layer that affect execution plans.
Apply in a development or quality system first, execute the affected Fiori apps or programs, verify correct results, and confirm the execution plan has changed as expected via M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE before promoting to production.

Manage the SQL Plan Cache Proactively

After applying any execution hint or changing ABAP logic, clear the relevant SQL Plan Cache entries.
Leaving stale entries in place will delay the effect of any optimization until the cache entry naturally expires.

-- Clear cache entries for a specific CDS view
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

-- Clear cache entries for a specific statement hash
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_HASH = 'your_statement_hash_here';

Monitor Regularly, Not Just Reactively

Set up routine monitoring for:

  • CPU usage trends over 7-day rolling windows - distinguishes growing load from isolated incidents.
  • Top SQL statements by CPU time via M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE or HANA Cockpit's SQL Monitor.
  • Out-of-Memory dumps - a leading indicator of memory pressure before they become business-impacting outages.

Early detection allows you to address performance issues during planned maintenance windows rather than under incident pressure.

Summary

Both issues share a common theme: code that looks correct and performs acceptably in development causes measurable production impact at scale.
The fixes are targeted, low-risk, and reversible.

IssueRoot CauseSolutionSAP Note
High CPU on CDS views with localeWITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) prevents HEX engine usageEnable hex_enable_with_parameters_locale (prerequisite), then add USE_HEX_PLAN via @consumption.dbHints (preferred) or ANNOTATION fallback

3513507, 3076534,
2717265

High CPU from large filter listsABAP IN operator generates thousands of SQL conditionsUse FOR ALL ENTRIES with FDA hint2399993

 

References

  • SAP Note 3513507 - HANA HEX Engine not used for queries containing WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?)
  • SAP Note 3076534 - Temporary use of ANNOTATION HINT
  • SAP Note 2399993 - FAQ: SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)
  • SAP Note 2717265 - ABAP CDS: Using Database Hints via Annotations (preferred approach for custom CDS views)
  • SAP Help Portal — Accessing Localized Texts in CDS Views
  • SAP HANA Administration Guide — SQL Plan Cache

entry.title: Enhancing Fiori Elements with Contact Quick View in RAP using CDS Annotation.
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

SAP Fiori Elements provides rich UI capabilities through annotations, reducing the need for custom UI development. One such feature is the Contact Quick View, which allows users to view contact information such as name, phone number, and address in a pop-up card directly from a table or object page.

In RAP applications, Contact Quick View can be implemented using CDS view annotations and UI metadata annotations. This blog shows how to create a contact entity and display the contact card in object page sections.

Why Contact Quick View?

A Contact Quick View typically act as a pop-up card that appears when a user clicks on a contact field in a Fiori application. It provides quick access to contact details without navigating to another screen.

Typical information displayed includes:

  • Full Name
  • Phone Number
  • Address
  • Email

This feature improves the user experience by making important contact information readily available.

STEP 1: Create a Contact CDS View.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Contact entity view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZI_CONTACT
{
  key id            as ID,

  @Semantics.name.fullName: true
  name              as Name,

  @Semantics.telephone.type: [#PREF]
  phone             as Phone,

  @Semantics.eMail.address: true
  email             as Email,

  @Semantics.address.label: true
  address_label     as AddressLabel
}

 

AnnotationPurpose
@Semantics.name.fullNameIdentifies the full name of the contact
@Semantics.telephone.typeDefines the preferred phone number
@Semantics.eMail.addressDefines the eMail address
@Semantics.address.labelAddress description

These semantic annotations enable SAP Fiori Elements to automatically build the Contact Quick View card.

STEP 2: Create an Association to the Contact Entity

In your root CDS view, create an association to the contact entity.

association [0..1] to ZI_CONTACT as _Contact 
on $projection.ContactID = _Contact.ID;

Expose the association:

_Conatct

STEP 3: Display Contact Quick View in Object Page.

To show the contact information in a Field Group or Header Section, use the #AS_CONTACT annotation.

@ui.facet: [ { purpose : #HEADER,
                 label : 'General Data',
                  type : #FIELDGROUP_REFERENCE,
       targetQualifier : 'HeaderData' } ]

@UI.fieldGroup: [ { qualifier : 'HeaderData',
                         type : #AS_CONTACT,
                        label : 'Contact Information',
                     position : 70,
                        value : '_Contact' } ]

When users open the Object Page, they will see a contact field. Clicking it opens the Contact Quick View card displaying all contact details.

To display the contact directly within a List Report table, use the following annotation:

@UI.lineItem: [
  {
    type      : #AS_CONTACT,
    label     : 'Contact',
    value     : '_Contact',
    position  : 120
  }
]

Output:

The Contact Quick View feature offers several advantages:

  • Improved user experience
  • Quick access to contact information
  • Reduced navigation effort
  • Standard SAP Fiori Elements functionality
  • Minimal custom UI development
  • Fully annotation-driven implementation

Key Points

  1. Contact data must be available through a dedicated CDS entity.
  2. Semantic annotations are mandatory for Fiori Elements to recognize contact information.
  3. The association to the contact entity must be exposed in the consumption view.
  4. Use type: #AS_CONTACT in @ui.lineItem for List Reports.
  5. Use type: #AS_CONTACT in @ui.fieldGroup for Object Pages.

Conclusion

Contact Quick View is a powerful Fiori Elements feature that enhances application usability by providing instant access to contact information. By leveraging CDS semantic annotations and the #AS_CONTACT UI annotation, RAP developers can implement this functionality with minimal effort and no custom UI coding.

Using this approach, SAP developers can deliver more interactive and user-friendly applications while fully embracing the annotation-driven development model of RAP and SAP Fiori Elements.

entry.title: Why SAP S/4HANA Transformations Fail When Business Processes Are Automated Before They Are Optimized
entry.content:
Why SAP S/4HANA Transformations Fail When Business Processes Are Automated Before They Are Optimized

Introduction

One of the most common mistakes I have observed during SAP S/4HANA transformation programs is the tendency to automate existing business processes without first evaluating whether those processes are effective.

Organizations often invest significant time and resources implementing automation, workflows, robotic process automation (RPA), AI-powered assistants, and system integrations. However, if the underlying process is inefficient, inconsistent, or poorly designed, automation may simply accelerate existing problems rather than solve them.

Over the years, I have seen organizations achieve greater transformation success when they focus first on process optimization and then on automation.

The Automation Trap

Many transformation programs begin with the goal of reducing manual effort.

While this objective is understandable, teams sometimes assume that every manual activity should be automated.

Questions such as:

  • Can this approval be automated?

  • Can AI make this decision?

  • Can we eliminate manual intervention?

are frequently discussed.

However, a more important question is often overlooked:

Is this process designed correctly in the first place?

Automating an inefficient process rarely creates an efficient outcome.

When Automation Creates New Problems

In one transformation program, multiple approval layers had evolved over several years.

The process contained redundant approvals, overlapping responsibilities, and unnecessary delays.

Rather than simplifying the process, the organization initially focused on automating the approval workflow.

The result was predictable.

Approvals moved electronically instead of manually, but the overall cycle time remained largely unchanged because the process itself was still inefficient.

The organization eventually redesigned the approval structure, eliminated unnecessary steps, clarified ownership, and then automated the optimized workflow.

Only then did measurable improvements occur.

Process Optimization Before Automation

Before automation initiatives begin, organizations should evaluate:

Process Complexity

Does the process contain unnecessary steps?

Process Ownership

Is accountability clearly defined?

Business Value

Does each activity contribute meaningful value?

Standardization

Are different teams following the same process?

Compliance Requirements

Which controls are truly required?

Answering these questions often reveals opportunities to simplify operations before introducing automation.

The Role of SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA provides powerful capabilities for process standardization and automation.

However, the platform delivers the greatest value when organizations use the transformation as an opportunity to challenge existing practices.

Instead of asking:

"What should we automate?"

Organizations should first ask:

"What should we improve?"

The most successful transformations typically combine process redesign, organizational alignment, and technology modernization.

Where AI Fits Into the Equation

The growing adoption of AI introduces another layer of opportunity.

AI can:

  • Identify process bottlenecks

  • Detect inefficiencies

  • Predict delays

  • Recommend workflow improvements

  • Analyze user behavior patterns

However, AI is most effective when applied to well-defined and optimized business processes.

Organizations that deploy AI on top of inconsistent processes often struggle to achieve expected outcomes.

Signs That a Process Needs Optimization First

Several indicators suggest that optimization should occur before automation:

  • Frequent exceptions

  • Multiple approval layers

  • Duplicate data entry

  • Unclear ownership

  • High rework rates

  • Inconsistent execution across teams

  • Long cycle times

When these symptoms exist, process redesign should generally precede automation initiatives.

Lessons Learned

One lesson I have learned throughout SAP transformation programs is that technology is rarely the root cause of process inefficiency.

Most challenges originate from process design, governance, ownership, or organizational complexity.

Automation can amplify the effectiveness of a good process.

It can also amplify the inefficiencies of a poor one.

The difference lies in whether optimization occurs before automation.

Final Thoughts

SAP S/4HANA transformations provide organizations with a unique opportunity to rethink how work is performed.

Rather than simply digitizing existing processes, organizations should use transformation programs to simplify, standardize, and optimize operations before introducing automation.

In my experience, the most successful SAP programs are not those that automate the most processes. They are the ones that improve the right processes first and then apply automation where it creates meaningful business value.

Optimization before automation is often the difference between transformation and digitized inefficiency.

entry.title: Building a Sales Order Backlog Report Using Custom CDS Views (Sales + Billing)
entry.content:
Amartya Ghosh Dastidar, Senior SAP Developer


Introduction

Core Data Services (CDS) views are central to data modelling in SAP S/4HANA. A frequent requirement in the Sales and Distribution (SD) area is a sales order backlog report — the value and quantity of customer orders that have been placed but not yet invoiced. This post explains how to build such a report using custom CDS views that combine the Sales and Billing domains, covering the key techniques involved: joins, aggregation, calculated fields, type casting, null handling, and the correct treatment of cancelled and pro-forma invoices.

Prerequisites

  • SAP S/4HANA system with ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse
  • Basic knowledge of ABAP CDS and SQL
  • Familiarity with SD documents (sales orders, billing documents) and the display transactions VA03 / VF03

Understanding the Sales Order Backlog

In SD, a customer order flows through a chain of documents before revenue is recognised:

Sales Order  --->  Outbound Delivery  --->  Billing Document

            (delivery backlog)        (billing backlog)

The term “backlog” can refer to either of two gaps in this chain:

  1. Delivery backlog — orders placed but not yet delivered.
  2. Billing backlog — orders placed but not yet invoiced.

The report described here implements the billing view, defined as Backlog = Ordered Value − Billed Value. This definition determines the data model, because it requires the Sales domain and the Billing domain to be combined rather than reading open order quantities alone.

Standard CDS Views Used

The report is built on top of two released standard CDS views:

  1. I_SalesOrderItem — sales order line items (ordered quantity and net value).
  2. I_BillingDocumentItem — billing document line items (invoiced quantity and net value), which reference the originating sales order line via SalesDocument and SalesDocumentItem.

Solution Architecture

The solution uses a two-view (layered) design:

  1. ZIC_LIVEBILLING (helper view) — returns only valid, non-cancelled invoices per sales order item.
  2. ZIC_SALES_BACKLOG (main view) — joins the sales order item to the helper, aggregates billing, and calculates the backlog.

The layering is necessary because the cancellation logic requires a self-join that cannot be expressed inline within an aggregating view on all CDS compiler releases. Isolating it in a helper view also keeps the cleaning logic in one place and results in a cleaner, more maintainable model.

Step 1: Create the Helper View (ZIC_LIVEBILLING)

This view filters the billing document items down to genuine, live invoices — excluding pro-forma documents, cancellation documents, and cancelled originals.

Explanation

  1. SDDocumentCategory = 'M' — keeps only invoice-type documents; excludes pro-forma invoices.
  2. CancelledBillingDocument = '' — excludes the cancellation documents themselves.
  3. The self-join on C.CancelledBillingDocument = B.BillingDocument links each invoice to any document that cancelled it. The condition C.BillingDocument is null then keeps only invoices that were never cancelled, removing the cancelled originals.

Step 2: Create the Main View (ZIC_SALES_BACKLOG)

The main view joins the sales order item to the cleaned billing data, aggregates the billed values, and derives the backlog.

Key Techniques Explained

  1. Aggregation with GROUP BY — a single order line can be billed across several invoices (partial billing). Summing the billing per order key collapses these into one row and prevents the ordered value from being multiplied.
  2. Type casting — currency amounts of different internal lengths cannot be subtracted directly. Casting all amounts to abap.curr(23,2) (and quantities to abap.quan(13,3)) ensures type compatibility.
  3. COALESCE for null handling — an order line with no invoice returns null from the join; coalesce(..., 0) converts null to zero so the arithmetic remains valid.
  4. Calculated fields — the backlog quantity and value are derived inline as ordered minus billed.
  5. Session date — dats_days_between(RequestedDeliveryDate, $session.system_date) produces the number of days a line is overdue, enabling aging analysis.

Business Logic: Counting Only Valid Billing

Accurate backlog depends on summing only genuine, live invoices. Three categories must be excluded:

  1. Pro-forma invoices — not real sales; excluded via SDDocumentCategory = 'M'.
  2. Cancelled invoices — a cancelled invoice and its cancellation document both remain in the table; the self-join in the helper view removes both, leaving only the live invoice.
  3. Rejected order items — lines with a rejection reason are excluded via SalesDocumentRjcnReason = ''.

Note: the field that stores the cancellation reference can vary by release and may sit on the cancellation document rather than the original. The self-join approach is robust to this, because it identifies cancelled documents from the relationship rather than from a single status field.

Validation

The view should be validated against the source documents (VA03, VF03, and document flow) across the main data patterns:

  • Never billed — no invoice in the flow; backlog equals the full order value.
  • Partially billed — invoice covers part of the order; backlog equals the remaining value and quantity.
  • Fully billed — invoice equals the order; backlog is zero.
  • Cancelled invoice — only the live invoice is counted; backlog reflects the remaining value.

Where source data itself is inconsistent (for example a negatively priced item or a mis-keyed invoice amount), the view reflects the source faithfully. A reporting view should not silently correct source data; such cases are data-quality issues to be flagged separately.

Standard vs Custom CDS Views

Standard delivery/open-order backlog content is appropriate where a vanilla delivery backlog with standard fields is required. A custom view is justified when the requirement differs, as in this case:

  • The metric is ordered − billed (a billing backlog), which standard content does not frame the same way.
  • It combines Sales and Billing into one pre-shaped row with the specific fields and aging logic required for reporting.
  • It encodes system-specific billing rules (pro-forma and cancellation handling) validated against actual documents.

The trade-off is ongoing maintenance. Where standard content satisfies the same definition, it should be preferred to reduce maintenance overhead.

Key Points to Remember

  • Clarify the backlog definition (delivery vs billing) before designing the view.
  • The SQL view name is limited to 16 characters.
  • Aggregate one-to-many relationships before joining to avoid duplication.
  • Cast all amounts and quantities to a common type before arithmetic.
  • Exclude pro-forma, cancelled, and rejected documents from billed totals.
  • Use a layered design when complex filtering cannot be expressed inline.
  • Validate the output against source documents before release.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Business Partner Address Management
entry.content:
Business Partner Address 
 
In SAP S/4HANA, the Business Partner (BP) acts as a centralized master data object. It serves as a single point of entry for managing all master data related to Customers, Vendors, and Suppliers. 

Unlike the older SAP ECC system, which requires separate transactions (XD01, VD01, XK01, etc.) to maintain separate customer and vendor master data, the Business Partner model in S/4HANA consolidates these entries. This helps avoid data redundancy and streamlines transaction processing. 

When we look into Business Partner Addresses, we are specifically dealing with the geographic location and communication contact details maintained for that particular BP. 

Major Types of Business Partners 

There are three primary categories of Business Partners in SAP : 

  • Person: Refers to an individual (e.g., a specific employee, private customer, or contact person). 
  • Organization: Refers to a legal entity or company (e.g., a corporate client, vendor, or subsidiary). 
  • Group: Refers to a collection of persons or organizations (e.g., a household or a buying consortium). 

 When it comes to Business Partner Address handling, the behavior is more or less similar across all BP types. 

However, there is a minor difference between Person BP and Organization BP. 
 
When creating an Organization BP, an address number is mandatory and automatically assigned to the data record.  

However, for a Person BP, a physical address number is not strictly mandatory at the time of creation. Instead, a unique person number is generated in the BUT000 table, which points to person-centric address details stored in the ADRP table. 

 
In the below Screenshot, have created a Person BP without Address. 

If you see the BP entry in table BUT000 (General Business Partner Data), you can observe the generated Person Number. 

Maintaining a Business Partner Address 

When creating or updating a Business Partner address, the following location details are generally maintained: 

  • Street 
  • House Number 
  • Postal Code 
  • City 
  • Country 
  • Region 

 
Mandatory Field Validation 

Country is a strictly mandatory field. 

If the Country field is not maintained, SAP throws the following standard validation error:  
"Address is incomplete; please enter Country/Region". 
 

Once the address is successfully maintained and saved, it becomes visible under the Address Overview tab of the Business Partner master data. 

Address Validity and the Standard Address 

 By default, when a new address is created, the system sets its validity period from the Date of Creation until the end of technical time (31/12/9999). This first valid address automatically becomes the Standard Address. 

 

Important Rule 

Every active Business Partner must contain at least one valid Standard Address. 

If the validity of the Standard Address expires and no new valid address exists, the system throws an error during transaction processing. 
The Standard Address is used as the default address in standard SAP transactions. 
 

The created address entries can also be seen in backend table BUT020. 

Communication Types in Business Partner 

Within a Business Partner record, communication details (such as Mobile Number, Telephone, Email Address, and Fax) are split into two categories: 

  1. Independent Communication 
  1. Dependent Communication 

 

Independent Communication 

These contact details are linked directly to the Business Partner as an entity, independent of any physical address. 

Example: Your personal mobile number or personal email ID that travels with you regardless of which office location you sit in. 

These communication details remain valid even if the physical address changes. 
 

The Address Number for Independent Communication is stored in table BUT000 under field ADDRCOMM. 

Since it is directly linked to the Business Partner, the communication reference is available in the General Business Partner Data table itself. 

To view the actual Email Address: 

  • Copy the ADDRCOMM value from BUT000. 
  • Search the same Address Number in table ADR6. 

 

Dependent Communication 

These contact details are explicitly linked to a specific physical address. 
 
Example: Imagine you are an employee at XYZ Company. You have a company-specific email ID linked to the corporate headquarters address. If you change companies or if that corporate office address changes, your company-specific email address becomes invalid, whereas your personal/independent communication details remain active. 
If the physical address changes or becomes invalid, the communication details associated with that address may also become invalid. 
 

The Address Number for Dependent Communication is stored in table BUT020. 

Since it is linked to a specific address, we need to fetch the Address Number from the BP Address table. 

To view the actual Email Address: 

  • Copy the Address Number from BUT020. 
  • Search the same Address Number in table ADR6.

 
Multiple Addresses & Address Usages 

A Single Business Partner can hold multiple addresses concurrently (e.g., a person can have a Residential Address, a Permanent Address, and a Work/Company Address). 

To manage how these addresses behave, SAP uses Address Usages. 

Examples: 

  • Billing Address 
  • Ship-To Address 
  • Standard Address 

A single address can simultaneously serve multiple purposes. 

Creating Multiple Addresses 

To create multiple addresses: 

  • Navigate to the Address Overview tab. 
  • Click on the Create button. 

Example, if we want to create a Billing Address: 

  • Double-click on Billing Address under Address Usages. 
  • Maintain the required address details. 

Note: SAP allows multiple addresses for the same usage type through SPRO configuration. 
 
SPRO Path 

SPRO → Cross-Application Components → SAP Business Partner → Business Partner → Basic Settings → Address Determination → Address Types 

Under this configuration, SAP provides a checkbox called Multiple Usages

If this checkbox is enabled: 

Multiple physical addresses can be assigned to the same Address Usage type.

 

All assigned Address Usages are stored in backend table BUT021_FS.

Address Programmatic Interface (BAPIs) 

When manipulating Business Partner addresses via custom ABAP code or interface developments, you should avoid direct database updates. Instead, use the following standard BAPIs: 

  • BUPA_ADDRESS_ADD — To append a new address to an existing BP. 
  • BUPA_ADDRESS_CHANGE — To modify existing address lines or fields. 
  • BUPA_ADDRESS_REMOVE — To delete or clear an address from a BP record. Etc... 

Conclusion 

Understanding SAP Business Partner Address Management and its backend table relationships is very important for both technical and functional consultants working in SAP S/4HANA environments. 

Having proper knowledge of: 

  • Address Validity 
  • Standard Address 
  • Independent and Dependent Communication 
  • Multiple Address Usage 
  • Backend Table Relationships 
  • Standard BP Address BAPIs 

helps significantly during: 

  • Support activities 
  • Incident analysis 
  • Debugging 
  • Interface development 
  • Custom enhancements 

I hope this blog provides a useful understanding of SAP Business Partner Address concepts and helps consultants working on real-time SAP BP scenarios.
